Figure 1: Expected gross and net payoff fixingeA$e_A$. Panel A plots gross payoffs for the board as a function ofeB$e_B$, holding constanteA>0$e_A>0$. The flat line presents the payoff for the fixed decision rule “chooseA$A$ifsA=H$s_A=H$and otherwise chooseB$B$.” The upward-sloping curve plots the payoff for the fixed decision rule “chooseB$B$ifsB=H$s_B=H$and otherwise chooseA$A$.” The bold curve reflects the payoff for an optimal decision rule, in which the decision depends upon both the signals and the efforts that generated them. Panel B plots payoffs net of the cost of effort. Since, in this example,eA$e_A$is large enough, the board's optimal effort to learn about candidateB$B$iseB=0$e_B=0$.
Figure 2: Optimal efforts and probability board chooses candidateA$A$. Panel A plots optimal efforts as a function of the priorQ$Q$in Solution 1,eA=(1−Q)QV$e_A=(1-Q)QV$andeB=0$ e_B=0$. WhenQ$Q$is closer to one-half, the board chooses higher effort because the uncertainty about ex ante quality is increasing. WhenQ$Q$is close to zero or one, the prior is nearly certain so the board exerts little effort. In Panel B, the solid curve plots the likelihood that the board chooses candidateA$A$if{eA,eB}={(1−Q)QV,0}$\lbrace e_{A},e_{B}\rbrace =\lbrace (1-Q)QV,0\rbrace$. WhenQ$Q$is small, asQ$Q$increases, the board exerts more effort to learn about candidateA$A$. A negative signal is more likely than a positive signal, so the likelihood that the board chooses candidateA$A$falls. AsQ$Q$approaches one-half, the likelihood of a bad signal falls to one-half, regardless of effort, so the likelihood thatA$A$is chosen increases back to one-half. A mirror-image process occurs whenQ>1/2$Q>1/2$. The dashed curve is the likelihood of choosingA$A$under Solution 2 and therefore is a reflection across the linePr(y=A)=1/2$\Pr (y=A)=1/2$. When the board is biased in favor of candidateA$A$, it will choose Solution 2 whenQ∈(0,1/2)$Q\in (0,1/2)$and will choose Solution 1 whenQ∈(1/2,1)$Q\in (1/2,1)$.Pr(y=A)>1/2$\text{Pr}(y=A)>1/2$, therefore, for allQ∈(0,1)$Q\in (0,1)$exceptQ=1/2$Q=1/2$.
Figure 3: Unequal priors. In Panel A, we plot the total efforts of the board, and in Panel B, we plot the likelihood that the board chooses candidateA$A$, both as a function ofQ$Q$forb→0$b\rightarrow 0$. IfQ$Q$is too small or too large, then the problem is degenerate and the board allocates no effort toward learning about either candidate. In this case, the board always selects candidateB$B$. If the problem is not degenerate, then (i) ifQ<11+z$Q<\frac{1}{1+z}$, the board allocates effort to learning about candidateB$B$but no effort to learning about candidateA$A$, and (ii) ifQ>11+z$Q>\frac{1}{1+z}$, then the board allocates effort to learning about candidateA$A$but no effort to learning about candidateB$B$. Since the board chooses the effort pair that maximizes the probability of selecting the preferred candidateA$A$, the probability that candidateA$A$is chosen is greater than one-half whenever both candidates are more likely to be bad than good(Q<1/2)$(Q<1/2)$or both candidates are more likely to be good than bad(Q>1/(2z))$(Q>1/(2z))$. These thresholds are indicated with vertical dashed lines.
